subtopics: Counting different things like animals, birds, trees and writing their number
Saying the sequence of numbers of things and people and writing them
Saying the number before, after and between the given numbrs upto 20
Arranging numbers upto 20 in ascending and descending orders and matching them
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on revising numbers from 1 to 20. Activities include counting various objects, sequencing numbers, identifying numbers before, after, and between given numbers, and arranging numbers in ascending and descending order.
original_exercises: Look at the following picture, count the different things in it and write their number.
How many huts are there?
How many children are there?
How many cows are grazing near the palm tree?
How many mangoes can you see on the tree?
How many parrots are there on the mango tree?
Which are more in number, parrots or mangoes?
Look at the picture given below. Answer the following questions.
What is the ordinal number of Basha? ....................
What is the ordinal number of Uma? ........................
Who is the second student?
Look at the picture given below. Write the ordinal number of the student shown.

topics.topic_name: Adding Pairs of Numbers Using Bundles of Sticks
topics.concept_summary: This exercise focuses on adding pairs of numbers using a visual aid - bundles of sticks. This method helps to understand the concept of addition by representing numbers with physical objects. By combining the bundles and individual sticks, children can visualize the sum of the two numbers.

topics.topic_name: Adding Numbers with Tens and Ones
topics.concept_summary: This exercise involves adding numbers by breaking them down into tens and ones. This method reinforces place value understanding and simplifies the addition process. By adding the tens and ones separately and then combining the results, children can develop a stronger grasp of addition.

This is a straightforward exercise in adding pairs of two-digit numbers. It builds upon the previous exercises and provides practice in applying addition skills. The variety of number pairs helps to reinforce addition concepts and improve fluency.

topics.topic_name: Adding Numbers and Identifying the Total
topics.concept_summary: This exercise involves adding numbers and then identifying the correct total from a given set of options. This tests both addition skills and the ability to recognize the correct answer. It encourages careful calculation and attention to detail.

topics.topic_name: Adding Numbers in Columns and Rows
topics.concept_summary: This exercise focuses on adding numbers arranged in columns and rows. It reinforces addition skills and introduces the concept of organizing numbers in a structured format. By adding numbers in both directions, children can develop a better understanding of addition and its properties.
original_exercises: A) Add 10
B) Add 14

topics.concept_summary: This section introduces the concept of oral addition, where numbers are added mentally without the aid of written calculations. It encourages mental math skills and develops number sense. The examples provided demonstrate different strategies for performing oral addition.
original_exercises: Example:` 25 G 14 R ?

topics.concept_summary: This exercise focuses on identifying pairs of numbers with different sums within a set of options. It reinforces addition skills and encourages critical thinking. By comparing the sums of different pairs, children can develop a better understanding of addition and its properties.

This section introduces the concept of addition with regrouping, also known as carrying over. It explains how to handle situations where the sum of digits in a column exceeds 9. The example demonstrates how to regroup ten ones into one ten and add it to the next column.
original_exercises: Say how much they have together.

topics.concept_summary: This section describes a game designed to reinforce addition skills in a fun and engaging way. The game involves picking number slips and adding the numbers, with the goal of having the lowest total to stay in the game. This encourages mental math, strategic thinking, and error identification.
This exercise focuses on understanding the concept of multiplication through repeated addition by counting fingers on hands. It reinforces the relationship between addition and multiplication.

subtopics: Writing multiplication tables (1 to 9) by using the method of successive addition
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on multiplication tables. Activities include writing multiplication tables (1 to 9) using successive addition.

subtopics: Writing the product of multiplying a two-digit number by a single-digit number.
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on multiplying numbers. Activities include writing the product of multiplying a two-digit number by a single-digit number.

chapters.chapter_title: Division of one number by another number

topics.topic_name: Division of one number by another number
subtopics: Identifying division and the symbol ' ÷ ' concerned.
Distributing a certain number of things among a certain number of people equally.
topics.concept_summary: This unit introduces division. Activities include identifying division and the symbol ' ÷ ', and distributing items equally among people.

subtopics: Measuring lengths using nonstandard measuring tools
Valuing the standard tools of measurment.
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on measuring length. Activities include measuring lengths using nonstandard tools and valuing standard measurement tools.
prerequisites: Division of one number by another number

topics.concept_summary: The provided text focuses on applying division to solve real-world problems involving distribution. It emphasizes understanding the context of each problem to determine what the result of the division indicates. The examples involve distributing items equally among groups, such as chocolates among friends or sheep among sons.

topics.concept_summary: The text introduces the concept of nonstandard measurement using cubits. It highlights the variability in measurements obtained by different individuals due to differences in their cubit length. The text encourages discussion on why these differences occur and prompts reflection on standard units of measurement used in games and other contexts.
original_exercises: Look at the pictures given below. Say how garlands of flowers are being measured.
How did everyone measure the garland with?
Why did each one get different measurement?
Among the three whose cubit is more?
Among the three whose cubit is less?
In general, what measurements do you use while playing games?

subtopics: Identifying the heavy and light things among things given
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on weight. Activities include identifying heavy and light objects.

The story illustrates the concept of weight through a donkey carrying salt and cotton bags. The donkey learns that the weight of the load changes depending on the material, especially when exposed to water. This helps to introduce the idea that different materials have different weights and react differently to external factors like water.
original_exercises: Guess the weights of two objects shown in each box.  Put a ‘9’ near the heavier object.
Guess the weights of two objects given together.  Put a ‘9’near the lighter object.

subtopics: Identifying the more and less size among those given.
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on measuring liquids. Activities include identifying larger and smaller quantities of liquids.

subtopics: Describing when things are done in a day (morning, afternoon etc)
Saying the names of days / months in a order
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on time. Activities include describing daily activities (morning, afternoon, etc.) and sequencing days and months.

subtopics: Identifying currency notes / coins
Giving change for notes less than Rs. 100
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on money. Activities include identifying currency notes and coins, and giving change for notes less than Rs. 100.

subtopics: Counting the things intens and ones. Saying how many tens and ones there are in them
Writing numbers upto 100 in the expanded form.
Writing numbers upto 100 in ascending and descending order and matching with things

Identifying numbers small and big relationship among
Solving puzzles based on certain conditions (More, Less)
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on numbers from 10 to 99. Activities include counting in tens and ones, writing numbers in expanded form, arranging numbers in ascending and descending order, identifying smaller and larger numbers, solving problems orally, and solving puzzles based on conditions like 'More' and 'Less'.

subtopics: Identifying tens
Identifying ones
Writing numbers based on tens and ones
topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on understanding the composition of numbers in terms of tens and ones. It reinforces the concept that a two-digit number is made up of a certain number of tens and a certain number of ones. The exercises aim to help students visualize and represent numbers using this structure.
original_exercises: Look at the number. Write how many tens there are in it?
Fill in the blank boxes with the correct numbers
Count the pictures in tens and ones. Write the correct numbers in the boxes.
Write the correct numbers in the blank boxes.
Write the correct numbers in the blank boxes.

subtopics: Identifying smaller numbers
Identifying bigger numbers
Identifying numbers within a range
topics.concept_summary: This section introduces the comparison of numbers. Students learn to identify smaller and bigger numbers. They also practice identifying numbers that fall within a specified range. These exercises build a foundation for understanding number order and relative magnitude.
original_exercises: Draw a ª μ around the smaller number. Look at the examples.
Put a ª9μ on the biggest number.
Draw a ª μ around the smallest number.
Identify between which numbers the given numbers lie with a ª9μ. Look at the example.

subtopics: Comparing amounts of money
Arranging numbers in ascending order
Identifying numbers based on place value
Creating word problems
topics.concept_summary: This section applies number concepts to problem-solving scenarios. Students compare amounts of money, arrange numbers in ascending order, and identify numbers based on their place value. They also create their own word problems, encouraging them to think critically about how numbers are used in real-life situations.
original_exercises: Ramesh has Rs. 50. Sita has Rs. 30. Who has more money? Answer orally.
Pavan got 45 marks in Mathematics, Janaki got 75, Razia got 65 and Vaani got 59. Say these numbers in ascending order.
Say the number in which 5 in ones place and 7 in tens place.
Say a problem which you can solve using the equation 20 + 5 = 25.

subtopics: Identifying numbers with a specific digit in the ones place
Following a path from smallest to largest number
Extending a sequence of numbers
topics.concept_summary: This section uses games and patterns to reinforce number concepts. Students identify numbers with a specific digit in the ones place and follow a path from the smallest to the largest number in a grid. These activities encourage strategic thinking and reinforce the understanding of number order.

- Show as many ways as you can.

subtopics: Length of the table
Distance between marbles
Length of garland of flowers
Length of thread
Distance in picture 5
Length of the room
topics.concept_summary: The pictures illustrate different scenarios where length needs to be measured. These scenarios include measuring the length of a table, the distance between marbles, the length of a garland of flowers, the length of a thread, the distance in a specific picture, and the length of a room. The exercise encourages observation and discussion about non-standard measuring tools used to determine length.

6. How is the length of the room measured?

topics.concept_summary: This exercise focuses on developing the ability to estimate length using a cubit and then comparing the estimation with the actual measured length. Pupils are expected to estimate the length of various objects, such as a table, blackboard, and classroom, using cubits and then measure the actual length to compare their estimations.

original_exercises: 1. Match the pictures with the numbers. One example is given.

subtopics: Understanding 100 as a combination of tens and ones
Identifying the smallest three-digit number
topics.concept_summary: This section introduces three-digit numbers, focusing on the number 100. It explains that 100 is the number that comes after 99 and is composed of 10 tens or 100 ones. It also highlights that 100 is the smallest three-digit number, building upon the understanding of two-digit numbers.
original_exercises: How many tens are there in 100? How many ones are there in 100?
How much is 99 + 1?

subtopics: Recognizing multiples of 100 up to 900
topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on counting in hundreds, introducing multiples of 100 from 100 to 900. The exercises aim to help students recognize and write these numbers, building a foundation for understanding larger three-digit numbers.
original_exercises: Count in hundreds. Write the correct number in blank boxes.

subtopics: Combining hundreds, tens, and ones
Reading numbers from 101 to 169
topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on reading three-digit numbers by combining hundreds, tens, and ones. It provides examples of numbers from 101 to 169, helping students understand how to read and interpret these numbers based on their place value components.
prerequisites: Understanding of hundreds, tens, and ones
original_exercises: Look at the bundles of sticks and the loose sticks. Read the numbers.

subtopics: Representing numbers using hundreds, tens, and ones
Writing numbers from 101 to 999
topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on writing three-digit numbers by representing them using hundreds, tens, and ones. It aims to help students understand how to write numbers from 101 to 999 based on their place value components.
prerequisites: Understanding of hundreds, tens, and ones
original_exercises: Look at the bundles of sticks and the loose sticks. Write the numbers in the blank boxes.
Look at the bundles of sticks and the loose sticks. Write the correct numbers in the blank boxes.

subtopics: Identifying the place of a digit in a number
Determining the place value of a digit
Determining the face value of a digit
Understanding the place value of zero
topics.concept_summary: This section introduces the concepts of place value and face value in three-digit numbers. It explains how to identify the place of a digit (hundreds, tens, or ones), determine its place value (e.g., 7 in the hundreds place has a place value of 700), and determine its face value (the digit itself). It also emphasizes that the place value of 0 is always 0, regardless of its position.
prerequisites: Understanding of hundreds, tens, and ones
original_exercises: Look at the following table. Write the place and place value of the digit.
Now write the place, place value and face value of the digits in 504.
What is the place of 0?
What is the place value?

subtopics: Relating numbers to currency notes and coins
Understanding expanded form of numbers
topics.concept_summary: This section connects numbers to real-world currency notes and coins. It explains how to represent a given amount using hundred-rupee notes, ten-rupee notes, and one-rupee coins. It also introduces the expanded form of numbers, showing how a number can be broken down into the sum of its place values.
prerequisites: Familiarity with currency notes and coins
original_exercises: Observe th following notes and coins. Count in Rs.100, Rs.10 and Re.1.
Write the given numbers in the expanded form. Look at the example.
Write the number in the short form.

subtopics: Understanding 1000 as 999 + 1
Understanding 1000 as a combination of hundreds, tens, and ones
Identifying the smallest four-digit number
topics.concept_summary: This section introduces four-digit numbers, focusing on the number 1000. It explains that 1000 is the number that comes after 999 and is composed of 10 hundreds, 100 tens, or 1000 ones. It also highlights that 1000 is the smallest four-digit number, building upon the understanding of three-digit numbers.
prerequisites: Understanding of hundreds, tens, and ones
original_exercises: Look at the currency notes and coins. Say how much you get if you add one to 999
How many 100s are there in a thousand?
How many 10s are there?
How many ones are there ?

subtopics: Filling in missing numbers in a grid
Identifying number patterns
topics.concept_summary: This section provides exercises where students fill in missing numbers in a grid. This helps reinforce their understanding of number sequences and patterns. The exercises cover different ranges of three-digit numbers.
original_exercises: Write the correct numbers in the blank boxes.

subtopics: Identifying the place of an underlined digit
Determining the place value of an underlined digit
topics.concept_summary: This section provides exercises to practice identifying the place and place value of digits in three-digit numbers. Students are given a number with a digit underlined and asked to identify its place (hundreds, tens, or ones) and its place value (e.g., 200, 40, 7).
original_exercises: Write the place and place value of the digit underlined in the number.

subtopics: Writing numbers in expanded form
topics.concept_summary: This section provides exercises to practice writing numbers in expanded form. Students are given a three-digit number and asked to break it down into the sum of its place values (e.g., 617 = 600 + 10 + 7).
original_exercises: Write the number in the expanded form.

topics.concept_summary: Not applicable, this section focuses on exercises.
original_exercises: Write numbers 1 to 4 for each object in the order of their increasing weight.
Mark the heavier of the two girls with a ‘9’

topics.concept_summary:
Ravi, Raju and Rama receive 2 litres of milk from three different people every day.
Rangamma pours 4 glassful of milk at Ravi’s house. At Raju’s house, milkmaid Vanajamma pours 2 mugful of milk. At Rama’s house milkman Hussain pours 8 small glassful of milk. The three children got a doubt on the milk supplied to each of them. Who is supplying the correct quantity of milk
original_exercises: Look at the pictures given below.  Say which vessels are used to measure milk.

topics.concept_summary: Not applicable, this section focuses on a game to understand estimation.
original_exercises:
Two children can play this game.
• Take a big bucket full of water.  Take a small bucket, a jug and a mug also.
• First, one pupil takes the jug and pours water from the big bucket into the small one.
• Then the second pupil also pours water with the mug into the small bucket.
• The pupils pour water alternatingly.
• Continue this till the small bucket is full.
• The pupil who fills the small bucket with jug or mug completely is the winner.

topics.concept_summary: Not applicable, this section focuses on exercises.

topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on comparing three-digit numbers. Activities include using >, <, = symbols to show smaller than about two numbers.

subtopics: Adding two digit numbers-both in expanded and short forms
Adding numbers less than 50 orally
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on the addition of numbers. Activities include adding two-digit numbers in both expanded and short forms, and adding numbers less than 50 orally.

subtopics: Adding two numbers using the 'carry over' method.
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on addition with carry-over. Activities include adding two numbers using the 'carry over' method.

subtopics: Subtraction of two digit number-both in expanded and  short forms.
Subtracting numbers upto 50 orally
Subtracting two numbers using the 'borrowing' method.
topics.concept_summary: This unit focuses on subtraction of numbers. Activities include subtracting two-digit numbers in both expanded and short forms, subtracting numbers up to 50 orally, and subtracting two numbers using the 'borrowing' method.

subtopics: Understanding that multiplication is nothing but successive addition of the same number
Saying the product of numbers related to numbers in columns and rows
topics.concept_summary: This unit introduces multiplication. Activities include understanding multiplication as successive addition and finding the product of numbers in columns and rows.

continues.  The one who lasts till the end is declared the winner.

picking sticks, other sticks are moved, the second player gets the chance.  In this
manner the two pupils play alternately.  The one who gets a bigger number scores
a point.

topics.concept_summary:
In 354 and 321 there are equal number of hundreds.
Now let us observe the tens.
In 354 there are 5 tens.
In 321 there are 2 tens.
There are more tens in 354 than 321.
Therefore 354 is bigger.
We say 354 is bigger than 321
We write     354  >  321
In the same manner 321 is less than 354
We write it as   321 <  354
In 3-digit numbers,
if the hundreds are
the same, the one
with more tens is
bigger.
original_exercises: Look at the notes and coins shown below. Say which are more and which are less.

topics.concept_summary:
In both 231 and 235, the hundreds and tens are equal.
Now observe the ones.
In 231 there are 1 ones.
In 235 there are 5 ones.
Therfore 235 is bigger.
It means 235 is bigger than 231.   235 > 231
231 is smaller than 235.           231 < 235
In three-digit
numbers, if
hundreds and tens
are equal, the one
with more ones is
the bigger number.

topics.concept_summary:
When one ten is changed into ones, 3 tens remain. Now if we add 10 ones and 5 ones,
If 27 ( 2 tens and 7 ones ) are subtracted from 45.
One ten and 8 ones remain. That is 18 remains.

5 ones are less than 7 ones
If we change 1 ten out of 4 tens into ones, and add to 5, we get 15.
15 ones  - 7 ones  =  8 ones
If one of the 4 tens changed into ones, we have 3 tens.
3 tens -2 tens  =  one ten.
or
1 ten  =  10 ones

topics.concept_summary:
Adding a number again and again is called repeated addition.
2 I 6 R 12—
6 I 2 R 12
Here we have used a symbol I. It is called the symbol for multiplication.
Multiplication is repeated addition.
Ex: 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 3 I 4 = 12

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on developing estimation and measurement skills using different vessels and water. The activity involves pouring water from one container to another to determine the capacity of each vessel. Students are encouraged to estimate the number of smaller containers of water needed to fill a larger one, and then verify their estimations through actual measurement.
original_exercises: Say how much water does each vessel hold.  Find out by actual measuring.

topics.concept_summary: This section introduces the concept of time and daily activities. It encourages students to observe pictures and discuss their daily routines from morning to night. The aim is to help them understand the sequence of events and associate specific activities with different times of the day.
original_exercises: Say what activities do you do in the morning.
Say what activities do you do in the afternoon.
Say what activities do you do in the evening.
Look at the pictures.  Say what things you do at what time.

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on understanding the days of the week. Students are prompted to observe the names of the days and answer questions related to the sequence and number of days in a week. This helps reinforce their knowledge of the weekly cycle.
original_exercises: (a) What is the day after Sunday?
(b) What is the day between Monday and Wednesday?
(c) What is the day after Saturday?

(d) After how many days does Friday comes
after Sunday?

(e) How many days are there from Monday to
Sunday?
(f) How many days are there in a week?

topics.concept_summary: This section introduces the months of the year and their sequence. Students are asked to observe the names of the months and answer questions about their order and intervals. This exercise aims to solidify their understanding of the annual cycle and the relative positions of different months.
original_exercises: (a) Which month comes after January?
(b) Which is the month between April and June?
(c) Which month comes after July?

(d) After how many months does December
come after September?
(e) How many months are there ?

topics.concept_summary: Understanding money involves identifying different currency notes and coins and recognizing their values. It also includes exploring different ways to provide change for a given amount using various combinations of notes and coins.
original_exercises: Look at the currency notes and coins.  Identify each one.  Say how much each one is.
Look at the coins shown below.  Say in how many other ways we can give change.
Look at the coins and notes shown below.  Say in how many other ways we can give change.
